natural_occurrence.description: Triphosphates do not occur naturally in free form. While phosphate compounds are essential in biology, the triphosphate salts used in food processing are synthetically manufactured.

manufacturing.description: Produced by heating a mixture of monosodium phosphate and disodium phosphate to approximately 540-560°C, causing condensation to form sodium triphosphate. The process involves controlled dehydration and polymerization of orthophosphates.

other_uses.cosmetics: Chelating agent and pH adjuster in personal care products.
other_uses.industrial: Heavy-duty detergents, water softening, ceramic production, paper processing, petroleum refining.
other_uses.household: Laundry detergents (historically common, now restricted in many regions due to environmental concerns about phosphates in wastewater).

eu.conditions: Quantum satis in many categories; specific limits apply due to concerns about total dietary phosphate intake.

japan.japanese_regulation_name: トリポリリン酸ナトリウム

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0-70 mg/kg bw/day (as phosphorus, for all phosphates)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: 40 mg/kg bw/day (as phosphorus, for all phosphates)

regulatory_history.event: EFSA's comprehensive evaluation of food phosphates expressed concern about high dietary intake from additives, particularly in Western processed food diets.
Many regions banned or restricted phosphates in laundry detergents due to eutrophication concerns (algae blooms in waterways), though food use remains approved.

commonly_found_in: Frozen shrimp and scallops (helps retain moisture during freezing)
fun_facts: Sodium tripolyphosphate can make seafood absorb up to 30% more water - this is why some frozen shrimp shrink dramatically when cooked
The same chemical used to make your frozen shrimp plump was once a major ingredient in laundry detergent until environmental bans
Triphosphates are particularly good at binding metal ions (chelating), which is why they prevent discoloration in processed foods
In the seafood industry, STPP-treated products must be labeled in many countries, as it increases weight (and price) through water retention
Despite the moisture retention benefits, excessive phosphate treatment can give seafood an unpleasant soapy or metallic taste
